channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Trump Hands NATO a Mixed Bag
item.description: The alliance wanted to look united. In walked Trump.

item.title: NATO survives its bipolar summit with Donald Trump
item.description: Allies breath a sigh of relief; Ukraine may even cheer

item.title: Elon Musk and the age of the corporate leviathan
item.description: For the world’s largest companies, the normal rules of corporate governance no longer apply

item.title: Is the U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire Over?
item.description: Trump calls continued peace talks a “waste of time” as strikes resume.

item.title: Modi Seeks to Project Influence on Asia-Pacific Trip
item.description: The Indian prime minister visits Indonesia, Australia, and New Zealand this week.

item.title: Is Donald Trump serious in declaring the ceasefire with Iran over?
item.description: Iran’s ultranationalist new leaders may welcome his bluster

item.title: Microsoft’s gaming strategy has misfired badly
item.description: A supply-chain crisis for Xbox couldn’t have come at a worse time

item.title: Propaganda Sustains Russians Fighting in Ukraine
item.description: Belief in Kremlin narratives dehumanizes Ukrainians and sustains the will to fight.

item.title: Can Zambia’s Historic Debt Swap Drive Economic Growth?
item.description: The country has agreed to invest interest savings in its electric grid.

item.title: How the biggest trees survive droughts
item.description: Being tall would be a disadvantage, if not for some clever engineering

item.title: How to hide from killer drones
item.description: In the Ukraine war, anti-AI tactics are producing bizarre forms of camouflage

item.title: A statistician’s guide to Wimbledon
item.description: How to turn a tiny advantage into sporting glory

item.title: The future of chipmaking looks more like Manhattan than Silicon Valley
item.description: Constrained by physics and politics, chipmakers are building upwards

item.title: Energy Markets’ False Dawn May Be Over
item.description: The U.S.-Iran deal has devolved into fighting again, with potentially ugly implications for oil prices.

item.title: Nigel Farage’s Path to Power Just Got Harder
item.description: The far-right U.K. leader has been hit by multiple scandals.

item.title: Russians are growing anxious and angry
item.description: The war has come home and is everyone’s problem

item.title: NATO’s New Enemy Is the Thermometer
item.description: Extreme heat events are threatening the alliance’s plans.

item.title: Brazilian cities are building vast surveillance networks
item.description: Can they combat the country’s biggest security threats?

item.title: What Will Happen to Iran’s Sea Mines?
item.description: The overlooked technology wreaked havoc on the Strait of Hormuz—and global trade.

item.title: How Argentina Can Protect Its Resource Wealth
item.description: Alaska, Norway, and Chile recognized the economic and political risks that accompany commodity booms and established sovereign wealth funds to preserve their resource revenues. Argentina’s hydrocarbon- and lithium-rich provinces should learn from their experience.

item.title: Reinventing Development Finance
item.description: Faced with shrinking concessional finance and rising borrowing costs, Rwanda has worked with the World Bank Group to leverage limited public funds and risk-sharing instruments to unlock private capital at scale and on affordable terms. It's an arrangement holding important lessons for low-income countries that must do more with less.

item.title: India, Indonesia Put in Place a Blueprint for a Durable Strategic Partnership
item.description: The highlight of the Indian prime minister's visit to Indonesia was the finalization of a long-pending agreement on the supply of BrahMos missile system and Astra air-to-air missiles.

item.title: Mamdani’s American Dream
item.description: New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s Independence Day address was a perfect example of ideology in the positive sense of the term. It overturned both the right-wing populist narrative of the United States and the left-wing "woke" critique with a vision that has the potential to mobilize a majority.

item.title: 4-Way Race for Democratic Party Leadership Tests Lee Jae-myung’s Influence
item.description: With Former Prime Minister Kim Min-seok’s formal confirmation of his bid for the leadership, the race may likely be intense, given the tensions between the pro-Lee and pro-Jung factions.

item.title: More and More of East Asia’s Youth Have Given up on Finding Jobs
item.description: A broken social bargain calls for a new approach to youth employment.

item.title: Three ways the LNG market could crack before winter
item.description: War, weather and outages may still send gas prices soaring

item.title: The Ankara Summit Declaration
item.description: Read the text outlining agreements at the 2026 NATO summit in Turkey.

item.title: Confronting Russia’s Shadow Fleet in Broad Daylight
item.description: The Kremlin is claiming and arming its smuggling fleet. European governments need to respond.

item.title: Taiwan and Poland’s Drone Industry Convergence
item.description: Insights from Dorota Maczuga

item.title: The New Constitution Reset the Presidential Clock in Kazakhstan. But Will Tokayev Run?
item.description: Kazakhstan's Constitutional Court confirmed this week that the president is eligible to run for another single seven-year term under the new constitution.

item.title: How Vulnerable Are US Financial Markets?
item.description: Fueled by more than $1 trillion in borrowed money and rising leverage across the financial system, US stock markets may have entered bubble territory. Two developments could trigger a sharp correction: higher interest rates and a loss of confidence in the handful of tech giants driving the AI boom.

item.title: The Global Energy Map Is Being Redrawn in Real Time
item.description: Confidence in the Strait of Hormuz has been shattered. Trust is now among the most important commodities in the energy world.

item.title: Concessional Finance Is Needed More Than Ever
item.description: As debt burdens rise and climate shocks intensify, sustainable financing mechanisms are becoming indispensable. The Islamic Development Bank’s new concessional fund offers a promising model for mobilizing the resources needed to protect development gains and invest in resilience.

item.title: Uzbekistan’s Race to Build a Digital State
item.description: As in many rapidly digitalizing countries, Uzbekistan’s technological transformation may be advancing faster than the necessary regulatory frameworks.

item.title: Three Decades in, China Is Cashing in on Sudan’s Collapse
item.description: China is playing a bigger game than aid and economic development.

item.title: Marco Rubio Burned Down the House to Fix a Sink
item.description: The secretary of state must be seen as the chief architect of the Trump administration’s assault on the rules-based order.

item.title: Middle East Dispatch: Mourning and spectacle in Iran
item.description: The point of the mega-funeral is to demonstrate the power of the regime, writes Anton La Guardia, our diplomatic editor

item.title: Despite Sanctions, the Myanmar Government Continues to Procure High Volumes of Jet Fuel
item.description: An independent group examined official documents from Myanmar ports, leaked bank documents and open-source data to chart the network that supplies Myanmar with jet fuel.

item.title: Why is India Allowing Chinese Equipment in Critical Government Projects Again?
item.description: Even sectors that are showcased as symbols of India's economic success often depend on Chinese inputs somewhere in the production chain.

item.title: Indonesian Documentary Breaks the Silence on the Plight of Indigenous Papuans
item.description: The recent film “Pig Feast: Colonialism in Our Times” has unsurprisingly elicited a strong negative reaction from the Indonesian government.

item.title: Ruling Class Clowns
item.description: By the end of the 19th-century in the US, it was clear to all interested observers that market power did not automatically or even easily translate into political capital and cultural authority. That is why, unlike the oligarchs of our time, then, certain Gilded Age capitalists learned to become a ruling class.

item.title: The Philippines’ ‘Trial of the Century’ Begins
item.description: A pall of cynicism hangs over proceedings against Vice President Sara Duterte.

item.title: The Ukraine Lesson Taiwan Keeps Missing
item.description: It’s not the drones—It’s everything around them.

item.title: Can the Private Sector Save Vietnam?
item.description: Why the Communist Party wants business to boom.
